content.problemStatement:
Dazzler has a blank canvas and (N−1)(N-1)(N−1) colours numbered from 222 to NNN.

Let BBB denote the beauty of the canvas. The beauty of a blank canvas is 111.

Dazzler paints the canvas by using all the (N−1)(N-1)(N−1) colours **exactly** once. On applying the ithi^{th}ith colour (2≤i≤N)(2 \leq i \leq N)(2≤i≤N):


If iii is odd, B=BB = BB=B &\&& iii.
If iii is even, B=B⊕iB = B \oplus iB=B⊕i.
Find the beauty of the canvas after applying all (N−1)(N-1)(N−1) colours.

**Note:** The colours are applied in **ascending order**. Colour number 222 is applied first. The ithi^{th}ith numbered colour is applied after (i−1)th(i-1)^{th}(i−1)th numbered colour for all i>2i \gt 2i>2.

Here &\&& and ⊕\oplus⊕ denote the bitwise AND and bitwise XOR operations respectively.


Input Format

First line will contain TTT, the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
Each test case contains of a single line of input, a single integer NNN.


Output Format
For each test case, output a single integer, the beauty of the canvas after applying all (N−1)(N-1)(N−1) colours.
content.inputFormat: First line contains T, the number of test cases. Each test case contains a single integer N.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the beauty of the canvas after applying all colours numbered from 2 up to N-1 inclusive.

sampleTestCases.explanation: N=2 → no colour is applied, result 1. N=3 → apply colour 2 (even): 1 xor 2 = 3. N=5 → apply colours 2,3,4: 1 xor 2 = 3, 3 & 3 = 3, 3 xor 4 = 7.
Using the derived formula: N=4 (mod 4 =0) → 3; N=6 (mod 4 =2) → N-1 =5; N=9 (mod 4 =1) → N+2 =11; N=10 (mod 4 =2) → N-1 =9.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved by observing the pattern of the beauty of the canvas after applying each colour. We can use the modulo operator to find the remainder of N when divided by 4, which determines the final beauty of the canvas.
solution.timeComplexity: O(1) per test case, O(T) total.
